coredata.dc:title: Non-cytotoxic lanthanum and nitrogen co-doped lignin-based carbon dots for selective detection of ions in biological imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xiaojun
Wu, Jialong
Wu, Manxiang
Wang, Lianfu
Yu, Dayu
Yan, Ning
Wu, Huiming
Zhu, Jin
Chen, Jing
coredata.dc:description: Owing to their versatility, fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted significant attention for applications in sensors, bioimaging, microfluidics, photodynamic therapy, drug delivery, light-emitting diode, etc. Herein, nitrogen and lanthanum co-doped multifunctional lignin-based carbon quantum dots ((N, La)-CQDs) were prepared from enzymatic hydrolysis lignin using a simple one-step hydrothermal method. The diameter of the CQDs obtained was about 2.2 nm with a good water solubility at the excitation wavelength of around 365 nm and emission wavelength of around 465 nm. This is the first discovery of (N, La)-CQDs to detect Sn2+. (N, La)-CQDs were successfully used to detect Fe3+, Sn2+, and ClO- ions in the range of 0–100 μM with the detection limits of 0.99 μM, 1.1 μM and 1.1 μM sequentially, and the linear fit R2 of 0.9997, 0.9943 and 0.9941 respectively. Non-cytotoxic (N, La)-CQDs can be used for labeling cells and detecting Sn2+ in zebrafish. These attractive features make these non-toxic, environmentally friendly CQDs material highly promising for applications in a wide range of areas, such as biomedicine, biosensing, disease diagnosis, and environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Ion detection
Non-cytotoxic
Biological imaging
Zebrafish imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram of (N, La)-CQDs preparation via hydrothermal treatment.
(a) TEM images of (N, La)-CQDs, (b) particle size distribution of (N, La)-CQDs, (c) XPS images of (N, La)-CQDs, (d) UV-Vis, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of (N, La)-CQDs.

Fluorescence spectra of (N, La)-CQDs containing Fe3+, Sn2+ and ClO- solutions (a1, b1, c1). The relationship between fluorescence intensity and ion concentration at 465 nm (a2, b2, c2). Linear relationship between the ion concentration and the fluorescence intensity of (N, La)-CQDs (a3, b3, c3).
Cytotoxicity of (a) L-CQDs, (b) N-CQDs, and (c) (N, La)-CQDs to 4T1 cells.
Confocal images of 4T1 cells cultured with the control group, 25 μg/mL L-CQDs, N-CQDS, and (N, La)-CQDs.
Confocal images of 4T1 cells cultured with (N, La)-CQDs at 0–25 μg/mL.
Fluorescence microscope imaging of zebrafish incubated with 25 μg/mL (N, La)-CQDs and the solution contained concentrations of Sn2+ are 0–25 μM.

head.ce:title: Non-cytotoxic lanthanum and nitrogen co-doped lignin-based carbon dots for selective detection of ions in biological imaging

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Owing to their versatility, fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted significant attention for applications in sensors, bioimaging, microfluidics, photodynamic therapy, drug delivery, light-emitting diode, etc. Herein, nitrogen and lanthanum co-doped multifunctional lignin-based carbon quantum dots ((N, La)-CQDs) were prepared from enzymatic hydrolysis lignin using a simple one-step hydrothermal method. The diameter of the CQDs obtained was about 2.2 nm with a good water solubility at the excitation wavelength of around 365 nm and emission wavelength of around 465 nm. This is the first discovery of (N, La)-CQDs to detect Sn2+. (N, La)-CQDs were successfully used to detect Fe3+, Sn2+, and ClO- ions in the range of 0–100 μM with the detection limits of 0.99 μM, 1.1 μM and 1.1 μM sequentially, and the linear fit R2 of 0.9997, 0.9943 and 0.9941 respectively. Non-cytotoxic (N, La)-CQDs can be used for labeling cells and detecting Sn2+ in zebrafish. These attractive features make these non-toxic, environmentally friendly CQDs material highly promising for applications in a wide range of areas, such as biomedicine, biosensing, disease diagnosis, and environmental monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Functional nanomaterials have attracted wide attention because of their application prospects in the biomedical field [1]. Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) are semiconductor nanoparticles with desirable photoelectric conversion [2–4], up-conversion luminescence [5–7], environmental friendliness [8], and high sensitivity characteristics [9], which are suited for usage in biological imaging, disease detection, drug delivery, disease treatment, etc. [10–13].

At present, carbon quantum dots are synthesized from either petroleum-based or bio-based raw materials. The main raw materials for making petroleum-based CQDs are phloroglucinol [6], m-phenylenediamine, o-phenylenediamine and p-phenylenediamine [14], ethylene glycol [15], etc. However, the synthesis methods from these raw materials require complicated purification steps that are also costly.

The main raw materials for producing bio-based carbon quantum dots are citric acid or citrate [16–18]，glucose [19,20], cellulose [21], lignin [22,23], chitosan [24], soybean [25], etc. Lignin is the second most abundant natural organic polymer on earth [26,27], but only a small amount of lignin is utilized for materials production commercially [28]. Wang et al. synthesized bright green fluorescent carbon quantum dots of 1.5–3.5 nm in size from alkali lignin by a one-pot method for ion detection, cell imaging, and electrocatalysis, but unfortunately, the detection limit and cytotoxicity performance need to be improved [29]. Shen et al. obtained CQDs consisting of carbon nuclei and surface functional groups with an average diameter of 4.86 nm in size by two-step hydrothermal treatment of o-amino benzene sulfonic acid and alkaline lignin at 200 °C for 16 h and used CQDs for Fe3+ detection. Their treatment method needed a long reaction time, which had a high energy demand [30]. Yu et al. made nitrogen-doped CQDs of 5.0 nm in size using diethylenetriamine and alkali lignin by a solvothermal method using a mixture of deionized water, ethanol, and isopropanol. The resulting CQDs showed good linear correlations for Fe3+ and Co2+ detection and exhibited excellent biocompatibility and multicolor cell imaging capabilities [31]. In addition, the preparation process for these CQDs was tedious that needed a long heating time and required large energy input. The processing methods used in these prior studies were also expensive. Moreover, no study has investigated the suitability of bio-based CQDs for the detection of pathogenic ions in the body, such as ClO- and Sn2+. These ions are essential elements for the organism, but deficiencies or excesses of ions can cause health problems. These ions play a very important role in many physiological and pathological processes. Thus, the feasibility of applying lignin-based functional nanomaterial for targeted labeling of pathogenic ions in the body needs to be investigated.

Fluorescent substances usually have a conjugation effect [32,33]. Lignin, the most abundant natural aromatic compound in the world [34,35], has a conjugated structure, and an increase in the number of conjugated structures can improve the fluorescence of CQDs. Therefore, lignin is a high-quality raw material for the preparation of CQDs, while giving a reuse method for the waste generated from paper production. Nitrogen-doped amino groups can also enhance the fluorescence intensity of CQDs [36–39]. Melamine contains both conjugated systems and amino acids [40], while the use of melamine-doped CQDs has not been studied yet. Metal elements can passivate the surface of CQDs to provide bright fluorescence, attributing to energy traps in passivated CQDs [41]. The hydrothermal method is one of the simple, economical and environmentally friendly methods of preparing CQDs.

In this study, we synthesized (N, La)-CQDs from enzymatic hydrolysis lignin, melamine, and lanthanum nitrate hexahydrate by a one-step hydrothermal method. Since melamine can provide amino groups and more conjugated systems while lanthanum can increase the fluorescence intensity, we passivated CQDs with melamine and lanthanum. The average size of obtained (N, La)-CQDs was 2.2 nm and the CQDs exhibited blue luminescence and good stability. The CQDs had good water solubility and cell membrane permeability with negligible cytotoxicity, which were used to detect Fe3+, Sn2+, and ClO- ions in cells. (N, La)-CQDs have also been shown to be non-cytotoxic and non-harmful to organisms, which can be used for real-time fluorescence imaging of mouse breast cancer cells (4T1 cells) and zebrafish, and for determination of ion concentrations in zebrafish., indicating their excellent potential and significance for practical bioimaging applications and in vivo ion detections.

All chemicals in this experiment are of reagent grade and used without any further puriﬁcation. Enzymatic hydrolysis lignin (EL), iron (II) chloride (FeCl2）, iron (III） chloride (FeCl3), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), lithium bromide (LiBr), stannous chloride dihydrate (SnCl2•2H2O), lanthanum nitrate hexahydrate (La(NO3)3•6H2O), potassium chloride (KCl), sodium chloride (NaCl), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), aluminum chloride (AlCl3), sodium hypochlorous acid solution (NaClO), and anhydrous calcium chloride (CaCl2) were purchased from Shandong Longli Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4•5H2O) and silver nitrate (AgNO3) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Fetal bovine serum and dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) were purchased from Wuhan Punosai Life Science and Technology Co., Ltd. Penicillin-Streptomycin liquid solution (double antibody) was purchased from Beijing Solexpo Technology Co., Ltd. 4T1 cells were purchased from Wuhan Shangen Biotechnology Co., Ltd. 4 % paraformaldehyde, phosphate buffered saline (PBS), cell counting kit-8 (cck-8 is used for the activity detection of some bioactive factors, large-scale anti-tumor drug screening, cytotoxicity test, cell proliferation test, and drug sensitivity test, etc.), zebrafish, medium (E3), and anesthetics (MS-222) were purchased from Shanghai FishBio Co., Ltd.

0.20 g of enzymatic hydrolysis lignin, 0.1 g of melamine and 50 µL of lanthanum nitrate (1 mol/L) and 60 mL deionized water were weighed and added in a 100 mL hydrothermal reactor. The product was heated in a blast oven at 180 °C for 5 h and cooled down naturally to room temperature for 12 h. The product was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min and then filtered through 0.22 microfiltration membrane. The CQDs solution was obtained. The CQDs with enzymatic hydrolysis lignin only were labelled as lignin-based carbon quantum dots (L-CQDs). The lignin-based CQDs with melamine but no lanthanum nitrate hexahydrate were labelled as nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs), and the lignin-based CQDs with melamine and lanthanum nitrate hexahydrate were labelled as nitrogen, lanthanum co-doped carbon quantum dots ((N, La)-CQDs). The resulting CQDs solutions were then dialyzed in a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 48 h to remove the unreacted chemicals from the solution [42]. The obtained product was stored without light at a temperature of 4 °C.

FeCl2, FeCl3, ZnCl2, LiBr, SnCl2•2H2O, KCl, NaCl, MgCl2, AlCl3, NaClO, CaCl2, NaH2PO4, CuSO4•5 H2O, and AgNO3 were made into 0.1 mM solutions, respectively. They were then stored in 100 mL volumetric flasks in a refrigerator at 4 ℃ for further use.

The metal cation test was performed by mixing 2 mL of Fe2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Li+, Sn2+, K+, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Ag+ with 1 mL of (N, La)-CQDs, respectively, for 30 s. The mixed solutions were placed on a quartz cuvette and their absorbance at 365 nm was measured using a UV spectrophotometer. To determine the stability of (N, La)-CQDs, each sample test was repeated 10 times shown in Fig. S1.

Similarly, the acid anion ion test was performed 10 times for each sample mixed by 2 mL of PO4 3-, Cl-, Br-, ClO-, SO4 2-, and NO3 - with 1 mL of (N, La)-CQDs, respectively, for measuring their absorbance at 365 nm.

Because (N, La)-CQDs were selective in detecting Fe3+ [2,43–49], Sn2+ [50–52] and ClO- [53–57], we performed fluorescence measurements of their solutions separately. 2 mL of aqueous solutions containing Fe3+, Sn2+ and ClO-, respectively (The concentrations of Fe3+, Sn2+ and ClO- were 2 μM, 4 μM, 6 μM, 8 μM, 10 μM, 20 μM, 40 μM, 60 μM, 80 μM, and 100 μM), were added into a quartz dish. 1 mL of (N, La)-CQDs solution was added to the same quartz dish and allowed to stand for 30 s. After the reaction was completed, the fluorescence absorption intensity was measured with a fluorescence spectrophotometer at 365 nm.

4T1 cells were used in high sugar DMEM containing 10 % fetal bovine serum and 1 % double antibody. The obtained liquid was incubated in a cell culture incubator at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 and 95 % air [58–60].

Using the cck-8 method [61,62], 100 µL cell suspension was prepared in a 96-well plate, which was incubated with 100 µL of different concentrations of CQDs (The concentrations of L-CQDs, N-CQDs, (N, La)-CQDs were 0 μg/mL, 5 μg/mL, 10 μg/mL, 15 μg/mL, 20 μg/mL, and 25 μg/mL, respectively) for 6 h. The cells were then incubated with 4T1 cells for 24 h, and afterwards washed with PBS for 3 times.

10 µL of cck-8 was added into the 96-well plate. The cells in the 96-well plate were incubated for another 1 h, and then the supernatant was removed. Colorimetric analysis was performed using an enzyme-labeled instrument. Each experiment was repeated for three times.

Cells were seeded in confocal dishes (1 ×105 cells/mL) and incubated for 24 h, after which different concentrations of CQDs (The concentrations of CQDs were 0 μg/mL, 5 μg/mL, 10 μg/mL, 15 μg/mL, 20 μg/mL, and 25 μg/mL, respectively) were added into the confocal dishes for 4 h. The experiment above was repeated for 3 times in order to show the repeatability. The cells were then washed 3 times with PBS (pH = 7.4) and imaged using a confocal laser scanning microscope [61,63] (illuminated with a 418 nm diode laser).

Sixty normally developing zebrafishes (72hpf) were selected in 60 mm Petri dishes with different concentrations of (N, La)-CQDs. After the zebrafishes were in contact with CQDs for 6 h. The culture solution was changed to deionized water and the zebrafishes continued to be incubated for 2 h [53,54,64–67].

The zebrafish were anesthetized with anesthetic and washed three times with PBS to remove the surface (N, La)-CQDs. Bright-field and fluorescence-field images of the zebrafish were taken using fluorescence microscopy to analyze the distribution and fluorescence intensity of (N, La)-CQDs in the zebrafish.

To test whether (N, La)-CQDs can be used for the detection of Fe3+, Sn2+ and ClO- in zebrafish, we incubated zebrafish with the (N, La)-CQDs solution for 6 h, followed by the addition of solutions containing Sn2+ (The concentrations of Sn2+ were 0 μM, 5 μM, 10 μM, 15 μM, 20 μM and 25 μM, respectively) for 2 h. Afterwards, the distribution and content of Sn2+ in the zebrafish were tested. The experiment above was repeated for 3 times to indicate the repeatability such as Fig. S2.

The morphology and size of CQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM2100, Japan). The elemental species of CQDs were detected on a C-O(α) radiation source of x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (AXIS SUPRA, Kratos UK). The functional groups of CQDs were analyzed by micro-Fourier transfer infrared spectroscopy (Cary 660 +620, USA) at room temperature with a resolution of 4.0 cm−1 for 32 scans recorded in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. The depth of CQDs was measured by a Scanning probe microscope (Dimension 3100, Vecco USA). The excitation and emission wavelengths and fluorescence intensities of CQDs were detected by UV-Vis NIR spectrophotometer (Lambda 950, US) and fluorescence spectrometer (FL3–111 HORIBA, France). The selectivity of CQDs for ions was studied by UV-Vis spectrophotometer (V-1800PC, China). Cytotoxicity of 4T1 cells was investigated by enzyme marker (cMax Plus, US). The distribution of CQDs and fluorescence intensity in cells were detected by laser confocal microscopy (TCS SP5, Germany) in the excitation wavelength of 405 nm. The distribution of CQDs and fluorescence intensity in zebrafish were tested by fluorescence microscopy (DM4B, China) in the excitation wavelength of 405 nm.

The (N, La)-CQDs were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal reaction of enzymatic hydrolysis lignin, melamine, and lanthanum nitrate, as shown in Fig. 1. Enzymatic hydrolysis lignin provided a rich carbon source for the synthesis of CQDs, while melamine and lanthanum nitrate contributed to the formation of surface functional groups and heteroatoms on CQDs. The resulting CQDs had good dispersibility and were easily soluble in water. As seen in Figs. S3a, b, the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs prepared without the addition of melamine and lanthanum nitrate, or with the addition of only either melamine or lanthanum nitrate, was relatively weak. However, the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs increased significantly when both melamine and lanthanum nitrate was added. By varying the quantity of the two additives, it was found that 0.10 g enzymatic hydrolysis lignin, 0.10 g melamine and 0.5 mL lanthanum nitrate (1 mol/L) addition level was the optimal condition. The obtained CQDs nanoparticles under this synthesis condition provided the strongest fluorescence intensity (Fig. S4).

Fig. 2a showed that the CQDs obtained were spherical, uniformly dispersed nanoparticles with particle sizes less than 10 nm [64,67], and a lattice spacing of 0.23 nm. As shown in Fig. 2b, the average particle size of the CQDs was about 2.2 nm. Scanning probe microscope traces of L-CQDs, N-CQDs, and (N, La)-CQDs particles were shown in Fig. S5, respectively. The height of the CQDs ranged from 2.0 nm to 7.0 nm [68], which matched the results from TEM measurements, indicating the accumulation of multiple carbon quantum dots.

Fig. 2c showed that (N, La)-CQDs were composed of four elements, i.e. C (1 s), N (1 s), O (1 s), and La (3d). It could be seen that there were only two elements (C element and O element) in L-CQDs. N-CQDs had an additional N element compared to L-CQDs. (N, La)-CQDs had one more La element in comparison to (N, La)-CQDs. It also showed that the nitrogen and lanthanum elements in the (N, La)-CQDs were uniformly distributed on the surface and matrix of the CQDs.

The surface groups were further confirmed by micro FT-IR (Fig. S6). FT-IR spectra of lignin, L-CQDs, N-CQDs, and (N, La)-CQDs showed some differences among these materials. There were mainly hydroxyl, amino, methylene, carbonyl, and carbon and nitrogen bonds seen in the spectrum for lignin [69]. The stretching vibrational peaks of hydroxyl (-OH) and amino (-NH) were at 3230–3415 cm−1 with the peak for -CH2 located at 2872 cm−1. In addition, the peak at 1573 cm−1 was attributed to the CO bond, while the peaks at 1321 cm−1 and 1155 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of the C-N bond. The peaks at 815 cm−1 was attributed to the bending vibration of N-H. The FT-IR spectrum of L-CQDs was not significantly different from that of lignin because the surface groups of the synthesized quantum dots were similar to those of lignin. The FT-IR spectrum of N-CQDs showed peaks at 3189 cm−1, 1639 cm−1 and 1427 cm−1, which was due to the N-H stretching vibration, CN double bond stretching vibration, and C-H bending vibration introduced by melamine. The FT-IR spectrum of (N, La)-CQDs showed a peak at 1315 cm−1 in comparison with that of N-CQDs, which was due to the inclusion of lanthanide ions.

Fluorescence spectra and UV-Vis absorption spectra are effective tools for characterizing optical properties of CQDs. The two most pronounced absorption peaks in the UV-Vis spectra of the prepared CQDs were located at 262 nm and 323 nm, as shown in Fig. 2d. The absorption peak at 262 nm was due to the π-π * leap of the CC bond [45] in sp. The peak at 323 nm was due to the n-π * leap of the conjugated CO bond [68]. As shown in Fig. 2d, the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of (N, La)-CQDs were 365 and 465 nm, respectively.

Due to their excellent luminescence properties and stability, (N, La)-CQDs can be used as fluorescent probes for the selective detection of metal ions (Fig. S7) and non-metal ions (Fig. S8) that is indicated by the detection of Fe3+, Sn2+ and ClO- in water. Especially, this is the first time we used (N, La)-CQDs to test Sn2+. Due to the greater affinity and effective coordination between the electron-deficient Fe3+ and Sn2+ and the electron-rich -COOH, -OH, and -NH2 groups on the surface of (N, La)-CQDs, this facilitated the initial electron leap and supported the transfer of electrons from CQDs to the half-filled 3D orbitals of Fe3+ and Sn2+, leading to non-radiative electron coordination to result in fluorescence quenching [45]. ClO- oxidized groups, such as NH2, on the surface of (N, La)-CQDs, into nitroso compounds to form non-fluorescent complexes [53,55–57]. Thus, (N, La)-CQDs could be used to monitor ClO-.

Fig. 3a1, b1, c1 illustrated the relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the ion concentration in solutions of Fe3+, Sn2+ and ClO- (The concentrations of Fe3+, Sn2+ and ClO- were 2 μM, 4 μM, 6 μM, 8 μM, 10 μM, 40 μM, 60 μM, 80 μM and 100 μM, respectively) with the addition of (N, La)-CQDs solutions. The fluorescence intensity decreased gradually with the increase in the ion concentration. Fig. 3a2, b2, c2 plotted fluorescence intensity at the maximum emission wavelength of 465 nm as a function of ion concentration. As shown in Fig. 3a3, b3, c3, the emission intensity of (N, La) -CQDs has a good linear relationship with the concentration of Fe3+, Sn2+ and ClO- when the concentration of Fe3+, Sn2+ and ClO- is less than 10 μM. When the concentration of Fe3+, Sn2+ and ClO - is less than 10 μM, the detection limits for Fe3+, Sn2+, and ClO- were 0.99 μM, 1.1 μM, and 1.1 μM and the linear fitting R2 values are 0.9997, 0.9943 and 0.9941, respectively.

The toxicity of (N, La)-CQDs to 4T1 cells was determined using CCK-8 before cell imaging experiments. As shown in Fig. 4, with the concentration of the CQDs in the range of 0–25 μg/mL, the cellular activity enhanced, indicating that these CQDs were non-cytotoxic. Due to the non-toxicity and excellent fluorescence properties of the CQDs, cell imaging experiments were conducted. Fig. 5 showed that 4T1 cells without CQDs had no fluorescence emission, while 4T1 cells treated with L-CQDs exhibited.

blue fluorescence (L-CQDs emission wavelength was 465 nm) but with low fluorescence intensity. The N-CQDs-treated 4T1 cells exhibited green fluorescence (N-CQDs emission wavelength was 505 nm) with also a low fluorescence intensity, and the (N, La)-CQDs-treated 4T1 cells exhibited blue fluorescence ((N, La)-CQDs emission wavelength was 465 nm) with a higher fluorescence intensity. L-CQDs, N-CQDs and (N, La)-CQDs were distributed in the cytoplasm.

As shown in Fig. 6, the fluorescence intensity of 4T1 cells increased as the concentration of (N, La)-CQDs increased from 0 to 25 μg/mL. The uniform distribution of blue fluorescence indicated that (N, La)-CQDs were uniformly distributed in the cytoplasm, which was caused by (N, La)-CQDs entering the cell through cytosol but failing to bind to the nucleus. Some of the darker cells in the figure were caused by the accumulation of cells as some cells were not in the same plane.

Various concentrations of (N, La)-CQDs were applied to transparent zebrafish embryos for in vivo bioimaging. Zebrafish imaging experiments were performed using different concentrations of (N, La)-CQDs in zebrafish after 6 h of incubation. Zebrafish were observed by fluorescence microscopy. It could be seen from Fig. S9, zebrafish incubated with (N, La)-CQDs showed a very clear blue fluorescence at 405 nm, with the fluorescence intensity gradually increasing as the concentration of the (N, La)-CQDs rose.

Zebrafishes were incubated for 6 h after the addition of the (N, La)-CQDs solution, and then the solution containing Sn2+ was added. The zebrafishes were incubated for another 2 h and they were shown in Fig. 7. From the experiment, it was found that zebrafish could absorb (N, La)-CQDs, which reduced the fluorescence of the entire zebrafish. The zebrafishes absorbed Sn2+ mainly through feeding, so the fluorescence intensity of the zebrafish abdomen decreased first.

In conclusion, (N, La)-CQDs were successfully prepared using a simple hydrothermal method from enzymatic hydrolysis lignin as the raw material. The (N, La)-CQDs were shown to be able to monitor changes in specific ions in water samples, live cells, and zebrafishes. (N, La)-CQDs were used as probes for the detection of Fe3+, Sn2+ and ClO- with the detection limits of 0.99 μM, 1.1 μM, and 1.1 μM respectively, and the linear fit R2 of 0.9997, 0.9943 and 0.9941 respectively. Using 4T1 cells as a cell model for cytotoxicity and cell imaging, (N, La)-CQDs were found to be non-toxic to 4T1 cells and emitted bright blue fluorescence in the cells. (N, La)-CQDs were also used for zebrafish embryo imaging and exhibited blue fluorescence inside the zebrafish. The fluorescence of the zebrafish containing (N, La)-CQDs gradually diminished upon the addition of an increasing concentration of Sn2+ solution. In summary, non-cytotoxic (N, La)-CQDs, a material that can selectively detect ions in living organisms and the aqueous environment, is one of the candidates in the medical field for cell imaging and ion detection.

Financial support from National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFE0102300), OCRIF Project #29983 (Canada), S&T Innovation 2025 Major Special Program of Ningbo (2022Z139), Foreign Cooperation Project of Fujian Province (2020I1005), National Science and Technology Project Record (2022L3028) are gratefully acknowledged.

Xiaojun Zhang & Jialong Wu contributed equally to this work and should be considered co-first authors. Jing Chen and Ning Yan conceived and designed this work. Jialong Wu, Manxiang Wu, Lianfu Wang and Huiming Wu conducted the experiments. Jialong Wu, Dayu Yu and Jin Zhu wrote and revised the manuscript. All authors participated in discussion and reviewed the manuscript before submission.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2023.109881.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
